cuisine: Singaporean — Indian Hawker (South Indian Tamil tradition)
region_of_origin: South India (Tamil Nadu, Kerala); adopted into Singapore and Malaysia hawker culture
food_subgroup: Deep-fried legume fritter — urad dal (black gram) ring fritter, hawker snack

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Vadai as a prepared hawker snack. Closest parent would be a fried legume product; no exact FoodOn term available as of 2026.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fried legume fritter (closest parent match — no exact FoodEx2 code for Medu Vadai)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0KCA used as closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for a fried legume fritter. No exact code for Medu Vadai exists in FoodEx2 as of 2026.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Vadai or fried lentil fritter as a prepared food item.

serving.description: 1 piece Medu Vadai (~60g), as served at Singapore Indian hawker stalls
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is one piece of Medu Vadai weighing approximately 60 grams, as typically sold at Singapore Indian hawker stalls and teh tarik shops, plain or accompanied by coconut chutney.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — Vadai / Fried Lentil Fritter; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Black Gram (Urad Dal), cooked, for micronutrient profile
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore + USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium) sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database for Vadai as a prepared hawker snack. Micronutrient profile (potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, phosphorus, zinc, vitamins) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for cooked black gram (urad dal) adjusted for deep-frying oil absorption. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy urad dal data with moderate confidence (0.9). All values are per 100g of prepared vadai.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Urad Dal (Black Gram) based preparations — analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database; peer-reviewed reference: Augustin et al. (2015) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Singapore hawker Medu Vadai found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated from the closest analogous food: urad dal (black gram) based preparations, which consistently show GI in the range 43–62 in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. The high protein (10g/100g) and dietary fibre (2.5g/100g) content of urad dal, combined with fat from deep-frying, moderates postprandial glucose response. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 18.0) / 100 = 9.9 for a 60g serving (18g carbohydrate).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 383mg/100g; approximately 230mg sodium per 60g piece
hypertension.guidance: One piece of Medu Vadai (~60g) contributes approximately 230mg sodium, which is moderate within a sodium-restricted diet target of 1500–2000mg/day; patients with hypertension should limit to one piece per snack occasion and avoid adding high-sodium condiments such as sambar.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~55) and low-moderate GL (~9.9 per 60g piece) due to urad dal protein and fibre content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Medu Vadai has a GI of approximately 55 and a glycemic load of approximately 9.9 per piece (60g), making it one of the lower-GI fried snack options for patients with Type 2 Diabetes; one piece as an occasional snack is acceptable within a carbohydrate-controlled meal plan, but deep-frying adds 15g fat/100g which should be accounted for in total energy intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation contributes 15g total fat/100g; saturated fat is relatively low at 2.5g/100g; cholesterol is 0mg (plant-based)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Medu Vadai is deep-fried and contributes approximately 9g total fat per 60g piece, with saturated fat of approximately 1.5g per piece and zero cholesterol; the saturated fat burden is lower than many animal-based fried snacks, but patients with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency to occasional consumption and prefer preparations using unsaturated frying oils such as sunflower or canola oil.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Folate (~36mcg per piece), iron (~1.3mg per piece), and quercetin from curry leaves
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Urad dal provides folate (~36mcg per 60g piece, approximately 9% RDA) and iron (~1.3mg per piece, approximately 7% RDA for males), which are relevant for RA-associated anaemia; the dish is vegan with no animal products and contains curry leaves which provide minor quercetin-based anti-inflammatory contribution at culinary doses.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Medu Vadai (also spelled Medhu Vadai, commonly called Vadai or Vada) is a popular South Indian savory fried snack deeply embedded in Singapore and Malaysia hawker food culture. It is made from urad dal (black gram lentils) that are soaked, ground into a thick batter, seasoned with curry leaves, onion, green chilli, ginger, cumin, and salt, shaped into a ring or doughnut form, and deep-fried in oil until golden brown and crispy on the outside while remaining soft and fluffy inside. One standard piece weighs approximately 60g and provides approximately 170 kcal, 6g protein, 9g fat, and 18g carbohydrate. The glycemic index is approximately 55 (low), and the glycemic load per piece is approximately 9.9 — making it one of the lower-GI fried snack options available at Singapore hawker centres. Sodium per piece is approximately 230mg. The dish is naturally vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal. It is commonly served at Indian hawker stalls, teh tarik shops, and mamak-style eateries for breakfast or tea time, accompanied by coconut chutney or a whole green chilli. Nutritionally, urad dal contributes plant protein, folate (60mcg/100g), iron (2.2mg/100g), magnesium (48mg/100g), and phosphorus (150mg/100g). Deep-frying adds fat but the saturated fat content remains relatively low at 2.5g/100g. Clinically, Medu Vadai is a reasonable occasional snack for patients with Type 2 Diabetes (low GI, moderate GL) and is lower in saturated fat than many animal-based fried snacks, though sodium and total fat intake should be monitored for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database for Vadai as a prepared hawker snack — an approved primary regional source for Singapore dishes. Micronutrient profile supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for cooked urad dal (black gram), an approved secondary global source. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous food (urad dal preparations, GI 43–62 range) with clear documentation of estimation basis. All mandatory multilingual names for a Singaporean dish are present. Confidence is high for macros and moderate-high for micronutrients.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for prepared Medu Vadai is not available in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation as of 2026. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for prepared deep-fried Medu Vadai is not reported in the primary sources consulted. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB Singapore source; traditional preparation uses palm or vegetable oil without hydrogenation. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; Medu Vadai is a plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; this is a vegan food with no animal-derived ingredients. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; no animal-derived or fortified ingredients present. 7. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database (urad dal preparations); no direct GI measurement for Singapore hawker Medu Vadai was found. 8. Copper (0.3mg/100g), manganese (0.8mg/100g), and vitamin K (2.0mcg/100g) values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy urad dal data with confidence 0.9. 9. The input record listed two default portions (50g and 100g); the canonical serving size has been set to 60g per piece as stated in the food name and description, consistent with Singapore hawker serving norms. 10. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (55 × 18.0g carbohydrate per 60g serving) / 100 = 9.9.
